title: The Macro Minute: Is the “Trump put” close to being activated?

How do team 42, it's your skipper here
at Darius Dale to present our macro
minute for Monday, March 24th, 2025. As
always, we'll start with the executive
summary from today's lead off morning
note. If you would like to review the
analysis supporting these conclusions
and to see if you need to make any
changes in your portfolio to remain on
the right side of market risk, you need
to be a client of 42 macro. So, today's
key macro question is, is the Trump put
close to being activated? Our answers
are, US stocks are off to a positive
start to the week with the S&P 500 and
Nasdaq 100 up over a percent a piece as
investors speculated on more nuanced,
less destructive tariff measures. Market
participants are drawing relief from
indications that Trump's so-called
liberation day tariffs may be more
surgical than the scorched earth
rhetoric he previously signaled.
According to administration sources, the
April 2nd rollout won't include
sector-specific tariff packages, calming
fears of an all-out trade war. The
tariff regime is shaping up to be
narrower in scope with exemptions for
some countries and no stacking of
existing steel or aluminum tariffs.
Still, uh President Trump wants fast
results with tariffs designed to hit
immediately upon announcement per White
House insiders. Despite internal back
and forth on execution, the president
remains among the most hawkish voices
within his administration pushing for
visible impact. April 2nd is going to be
a liberation day for America, uh
President Trump declared touting tens of
billions, potentially trillions in
tariff revenue over time. We've
carefully studied every meaningful
interview with key members of the
cabinet over the past few months and our
analysis leads us to conclude the strike
price of the Trump put is much lower
than where markets are currently pricing
it today.
Shifting gears, uh happy dot-com bubble
anniversary. Uh 25 years ago on March
24th, 2000, uh the S&P 500 hit a peak
that it wouldn't reclaim until 2007.
Three days before the Nasdaq 100 hit its
dot-com bubble high that it wouldn't
reclaim for 15 years.
The 1995 to 2000 bull run saw the S&P
500 nearly triple and the Nasdaq Nasdaq
100 surge nearly 718%
only to crash wiping out 80% of the
Nasdaq's value by 2002. Uh if you were
still overweight US stocks in March
2000, like most investors were, what
lessons did you learn from that market
cycle that you can apply to today's
market cycle?
Transitioning to our 42 macro dashboard,
as always, we'll wrap up with a question
here from our community. Uh this one's
regarding failed Treasury auction. Says,
"Hi, I just watched an interview with
Soros Fund CIO Don Fitzpatrick. Towards
the end, she says that it's possible
within the next couple of years uh that
a failed Treasury auction from uh an EU
country uh that a failed Treasury
auction from an EU country." And she
gives uh France and UK as possible
countries given their high levels of
debt. She could just be talking up there
uh up the the fund's own position, but
there isn't any precedence for something
like this uh to happen. Uh so, yeah, we
definitely saw it in the fall of 2022
with the Liz Truss moment. Uh you know,
we essentially saw the the gilt market
melt down uh and and it basically got no
bid uh for a brief period of time until
the Bank of England came in and stepped
in uh and started expanding its balance
sheet again. Um you know, I think the
risk of a failed Treasury auction is
probably higher in the US just given our
more precarious uh starting point. Uh we
have higher debt than most of those
European countries in terms of debt to
GDP ratios. Uh they certainly look at it
on a debt to liquidity basis where you
know, we're kind of in no man's land on
that metric relative to the rest of our
peer economies. Uh and then obviously
you look at our deficit uh which is
currently tracking at uh a little bit
over 7% uh of GDP. Now, obviously the
Trump administration and specifically
Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has uh
you know, sort of uh authored some
guidance that they would love to get the
deficit down to 3 percentage points of
GDP, uh but the reality is is one, they
can't do that overnight uh without
causing a recession in the economy. Uh
and two, it's not clear they're going to
be able to do that anyway when you
factor in uh some of the dynamics that
we're seeing with respect to the
extension and expansion of the Trump tax
cuts.
You know, you essentially have the
scenario where Dodge, at least according
to our estimates, is going to wind up
cutting about 500 billion to a trillion
dollars out of annual uh federal
expenditures. Meanwhile, you're layering
on about, you know, kind of four to to
to to four to five trillion dollars of
additional uh lost revenues in the
context of the tax cuts relative to the
current law baseline, which has most of
the provisions within the Trump tax cuts
um expiring on um on December 31st of
this year. Uh not the least of those
provisions are no tax on tips, no tax on
overtime pay. They're going to have to
increase the SALT deduction in order to
get the salty Republicans to play ball.
So, uh you have a lot more um
revenue reduction coming down the pipe
from a legislative standpoint than you
have from a deficit reduction
standpoint. So, investors should be
aware of that um in terms of in terms of
the net impact that they may have on the
budget deficit. Now, obviously the
President Trump administration, on
balance, is calling for a pro-growth
administration where deregulation,
uh tax cuts, and accelerated energy
production are all things that could
contribute to a high a faster potential
growth. They're essentially calling for
3 plus percent growth uh when you think
about their dynamic scoring of their of
their budget um resolution that'll feed
into the reconciliation process. Whereas
most bipartisan budget think tanks, you
know, you know, they're they're saying,
you know, the the the impact on growth
from from these policies to not be as
positive. It is net positive, but it's
it's much less net positive. I want to
say that when we did the analysis
looking at the seven largest kind of
most well-known bipartisan think tanks
uh on deficit and matters, uh their
median estimate for how much um
how much did the dynamic scoring would
ultimately impact positive growth in
revenues relative to the baseline is
somewhere around 200 billion. Whereas
the House Republicans uh estimate
somewhere around 2.6 trillion. So,
there's obviously a huge dichotomy
relative to where I would say non uh
folks who aren't incentivized to just
sort of tell President Trump what he
wants to hear uh
relative to the people who are
incentivized to do that. Uh and then one
final thing I'll say is um on the budget
deficit stuff that may increase the risk
of a Treasury a failed Treasury auction
at some point in the in the future. Um
if the Senate decides to go with the
current law baseline
as uh sorry, current policy baseline if
with respect to scoring their um the
reconciliation package as opposed to the
current law baseline, the current policy
baseline essentially assumes the the
cost of extending uh the current law
Trump tax cuts, which again expire on
December 31st of this year, uh as zero.
Which obviously free up trillions of
dollars of additional, you know, kind of
um tax cuts that they can layer on into
the into the program. And so, you know,
you're talking about
the potentiality for US debt to GDP to
be somewhere around 150% you know, kind
of in a in a 10-year kind of budget
window in the worst case scenario, but
certainly looking at about 130% you
know, up from about 105-ish 110%
currently. So, you know, these are
things these are dynamics that we all
continue to see. Um you know, I don't
think the the Treasury market is overly
concerned about them right now and they
shouldn't be because clearly, in our
opinion, we have a faster than expected
growth slowdown ahead of us that has has
to get priced in in our opinion. Uh but
ultimately, when we get to the other
side of uh the legislation of the
extension and expansion of the Trump tax
cuts um and and and and kind of just
move forward in time, the risk of of of
seeing a failed Treasury auction here in
the US uh will, in our opinion, will be
be quite high as we move forward in time
over the next uh few years because
ultimately, uh we just think the bond
market is is very inadequately priced
for the amount of supply that's probably
going to come down the pike. And and one
of the most important dynamics um that
is contributing to the risk of a of a
potential failed Treasury auction in
subsequent years is the fact that the
private non-bank sector, which used to
own 36% of Treasuries at the end of
2021, now owns 57% of marketable
Treasuries and that share continues to
rise. So, eventually, we, the private
sector, will buckle under the weight of
of Uncle Sam, especially if Uncle Sam
continues to get fed uh
as as you know,
if Uncle Sam's weight continues to go up
as as it likely would in the context of
trillions of dollars of tax cuts with
not the same level of spending cuts to
offset that. So, we're wrapping up
there. Hopefully everyone understood
that and benefited from that. Nothing to
do here today on this, but I do think
it's a very important um
the topic for our time given that we are
in a fourth turning. And typically, you
see some pretty exciting things both
economically and within financial
markets uh late in the fourth turning.
So, wrap it up there. Everyone, have a
wonderful day. Darius Dale here
presenting our macro minute for Monday,
March 24th, 2025. Best of luck out
there. We'll catch back here tomorrow.
